data.title: Bank of Hawaii: Earnings Recovery Ahead But Valuation Is Unattractive
data.description:
Earnings of Bank of Hawaii Corporation (NYSE:BOH) will most likely benefit in upcoming quarters from subdued loan growth. Moreover, the margin will likely recover somewhat thanks to reinvestment of cash flows from maturing fixed-rate loans. Overall, I’m expecting earnings to fall by 19% to $3.36 per share in 2024 and then rise by 7% to $3.60 per share. The December 2025 target price is below the current market price. Further, the company is offering a dividend yield of 4.3%. Based on the total expected return, I’m adopting a hold rating on Bank of Hawaii Corporation.

Reinvestments to Lift the Margin

After declining by 26 basis points in 2023 and 2 basis points in the first quarter of 2024, the net interest margin expanded by 4 basis points in the second quarter of this year. Management mentioned on the conference call that it expects the net interest margin to be flat to slightly higher in the second half of the year.

Management’s optimism is attributable to the fixed-rate and adjustable-rate loans maturing in the short term. The spread between the runoff yields and new yields was 3% in the second quarter, as mentioned on the conference call. Management expects new asset yields to continue to be well in excess of runoff yields. Further, it expects the annual cash flows from maturities and paydowns for the fixed rate and adjustable rate assets to be approximately $2.4 billion. As per my calculations given below, the reinvestment of outgoing assets could raise the average earning asset yield by 28 basis points.

Impact of Reinvestment of Maturing/Repricing Assets
Maturing/Repricing Assets a 2,400 USD million
Total Earning Assets b 21,425 USD million
Percentage of Assets Maturing c = (a/b) x 100 11.2% Percent
Difference B/w New and Outgoing Yields in 2Q d 300 basis points
Fed Funds Rate Cut in September e 50 basis points
Expected Diff Between New & Outgoing Yields f = d - e 250 basis points
Change in Average Earning Asset Yield g = f x c 28 basis points

Source: Analyst's Estimates and Calculations

While the rates on fixed and adjustable loans will rise, the rates on variable-rate loans will fall due to interest rate cuts. Therefore, the average asset yield will likely rise by far less than the 28 basis points I have calculated above.

On the plus side, the average deposit cost will fall due to interest rate cuts, which will help the margin. Variable-rate deposits, namely interest-bearing demand and savings deposits made up 59% of total deposits at the end of June. This means a majority of deposits will re-price downwards soon after every rate cut.

I’m expecting the Fed to cut the Fed funds rate by 75 basis points in 2024 (including the 50 basis points cut in September) and 75-100 basis points in 2025.

Considering these factors, I’m estimating the margin to have grown by 3 basis points in the third quarter of 2024. Further, I’m expecting the margin to grow by 2 basis points every quarter through the end of 2025.

Despite the positive outlook, the average margin for 2024 will still be below the average margin for 2023 because the anticipated recovery won’t be as sharp as the fall last year.

Loan Growth to Turn Around On the Back of Macroeconomic Factors

Bank of Hawaii’s loan portfolio continued to decline in the second quarter of this year. The portfolio dipped by 1.0% during the first half of 2024, or 1.9% annualized. This performance is quite unusual for the Bank of Hawaii, as its loan portfolio has grown at a compounded annual growth rate of 6.0% over the last five years. Moreover, the portfolio has grown by low to mid-single digits in most years in the past. As mentioned in the earnings release, the decline was due to decreases in the commercial mortgage, residential mortgage, home equity, and automobile portfolios.

I think loan growth can turn around and move closer to the historical average, partly because of interest rate cuts. Lower borrowing costs should whet the appetites of home buyers.

Additionally, the company’s markets currently seem strong economically. Around 92% of the company’s loans are to businesses and individuals in Hawaii. The state’s economy relies heavily on tourism. According to the Hawaii Government’s Department of Business, Economic Development & Tourism, the passenger count this year has been better than in previous years, but still not as good as 2019 (pre-pandemic).

Moreover, the state’s unemployment continues to be quite low, as shown below.

Further, the Economic Research Organization at the University of Hawaii projects the unemployment rate to continue to remain stable at the current level in the future.

Considering these factors, I’m estimating the loan portfolio to have grown by 1% in the third quarter of 2024. Further, I’m expecting the portfolio to grow by 1% every quarter through the end of 2025. I'm also expecting deposits to grow in line with loans. The following table shows my balance sheet estimates.

Financial Position FY19 FY20 FY21 FY22 FY23 FY24E FY25E
Net Loans 10,881 11,724 12,101 13,502 13,819 13,959 14,526
Growth of Net Loans 5.2% 7.7% 3.2% 11.6% 2.3% 1.0% 4.1%
Other Earning Assets 5,960 7,472 9,362 8,345 8,102 7,819 7,976
Deposits 15,784 18,212 20,360 20,616 21,055 20,819 21,664
Borrowings and Sub-Debt 798 768 564 1,136 711 667 681
Common equity 1,287 1,375 1,432 1,137 1,234 1,279 1,231
Book Value Per Share ($) 31.7 34.5 35.7 28.6 31.3 32.3 31.1
Tangible BVPS ($) 30.9 33.7 35.0 27.8 30.5 31.5 30.3

Source: SEC Filings, Analyst's Estimates

(In USD million unless otherwise specified)

Expecting the EPS to Grow by 7% to $3.60 Next Year

The anticipated loan growth will lift earnings in the future. However, the earnings for 2024 will likely still be below last year’s earnings because the average margin will be lower. Additionally, I’m making the following assumptions.

  1. I’m expecting the provisioning expense for expected loan losses to remain rangebound near last year’s level. This is because I’m expecting the economy to remain stable.
  2. I’m following management’s guidance for non-interest income. Management mentioned in the conference call that it expects core non-interest income to be slightly higher in the second half of the year as market conditions improve.
  3. The second quarter's non-interest expense had a number of one-off items that won’t recur in the future, including an FDIC special assessment of $2.6 million, $0.8 million of severance expenses, and $0.6 million of other expenses. Management mentioned in the conference call that it expects normalized expenses in 2024 to increase 1% to 2% from 2023 normalized expenses of $419 million. As this target seems reasonable to me, I have decided to take management’s guidance for 2024 and extrapolate it to next year.
  4. I have decided to take management’s guidance for the tax rate as well, which management expects to be around 24.5%.

Overall, I’m expecting earnings to drop by 19% to $3.36 per share in 2024 and rise by 7% to $3.60 per share in 2025. The following table shows my income statement estimates.

Income Statement FY19 FY20 FY21 FY22 FY23 FY24E FY25E
Net interest income 498 496 497 541 497 464 493
Provision for loan losses 16 118 (51) (8) 9 9 10
Non-interest income 183 184 171 158 177 169 172
Non-interest expense 379 374 394 415 438 437 457
Net income - Common Sh. 226 154 250 218 163 133 143
EPS - Diluted ($) 5.56 3.86 6.25 5.48 4.14 3.36 3.60

Source: SEC Filings, Earnings Releases, Analyst's Estimates

(In USD million unless otherwise specified)

Risks Stem from Location

The biggest source of risk is the geographical concentration of the Bank of Hawaii’s balance sheet. To top it off, Hawaii’s economy is also concentrated in just one industry: tourism.

Further, the company’s asset quality has recently worsened. Criticized loans rose to 2.23% of total loans by the end of June from 1.97% of total loans at the end of March 2024.

Additionally, uninsured and uncollateralized deposits made up 41% of total deposits at the end of June 2024, as mentioned in the presentation/financial report.

One factor that reduces the company’s riskiness is the more-than-adequate capital position. Bank of Hawaii reported a total capital ratio of 15.05% in the presentation, which is much higher than the minimum regulatory requirement of 10.50%.

In general, I think the Bank of Hawaii’s risk level is low to moderate.

Maintaining a Hold Rating

Bank of Hawaii is offering a dividend yield of 4.3%, assuming the company maintains its quarterly dividend at the current level of $0.70 per share. The company has maintained this dividend level since the second quarter of 2021; therefore, I believe it’s unlikely to change the dividend any time soon. The earnings and dividend estimates suggest a payout ratio of 78% for 2025, which is affordable even if it's higher than the five-year average of 56%.

I’m using the historical price-to-tangible book (P/TB) and price-to-earnings (P/E) multiples to value the Bank of Hawaii. The stock has traded at an average P/TB ratio of 2.326x and an average P/E ratio of around 14.53x in the past, as shown below.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with the forecast tangible book value per share of $30.30 gives a target price of $70.40 for the end of 2025. This price target implies a 7.8% upside from the October 22 closing price. The following table shows the sensitivity of the target price to the P/TB ratio.

P/TB Multiple 1.93x 2.13x 2.33x 2.53x 2.73x
TBVPS - Dec 2025 ($) 30.3 30.3 30.3 30.3 30.3
Target Price ($) 58.3 64.3 70.4 76.5 82.5
Market Price ($) 65.3 65.3 65.3 65.3 65.3
Upside/(Downside) (10.8)% (1.5)% 7.8% 17.0% 26.3%

Source: Analyst’s Estimates

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with the forecast earnings per share of $3.60 gives a target price of $52.30 for the end of 2025. This price target implies a 20.0% downside from the October 22 closing price. The following table shows the sensitivity of the target price to the P/E ratio.

P/E Multiple 12.5x 13.5x 14.5x 15.5x 16.5x
EPS 2025 ($) 3.60 3.60 3.60 3.60 3.60
Target Price ($) 45.1 48.7 52.3 55.9 59.5
Market Price ($) 65.3 65.3 65.3 65.3 65.3
Upside/(Downside) (31.0)% (25.5)% (20.0)% (14.5)% (9.0)%

Source: Analyst’s Estimates

Equally weighting the target prices from the two valuation methods gives a combined target price of $61.30, which implies a 6.1% downside from the current market price. Adding the forward dividend yield gives a total expected return of negative 1.8%.

I last covered Bank of Hawaii in January 2023 and adopted a hold rating. Based on my updated total expected return, I’m adopting a hold rating once again on Bank of Hawaii Corporation.

data.title: McDonald's Q3 Preview: Don't Worry About E. Coli, It's An Automation Play
data.description:
Co-Authored By Noah Cox and Brock Heilig.

Investment Thesis

While McDonald's Corporation (NYSE:MCD) shares are selling off today on the news of an E. Coli outbreak at stores across the US, shares overall have staged a strong recovery since the middle of the summer. US consumers have proven to be more resilient than expected.

Powering this, McDonald’s has launched a new series of value deals on their menu that is driving foot traffic at its stores. So far, it seems to be working.

However, getting consumers into the stores to buy these value deals is only part of the story. In order for McDonald’s to actually increase margins via these value items, the fast food chain needs to prioritize optimization and automation. That’s precisely what they’re doing.

The fast food chain has invested heavily in automation in multiple areas of their Golden Arch locations to help speed up operations and minimize labor cost.

While former President Donald Trump did serve fries in the drive-thru window at a McDonald’s location in Pennsylvania this past week, there is no guarantee that this will be the future of fast food work. I am bullish on the fast food chain.

McDonald's should benefit even further from the automation it is implementing. I believe that shares are a strong buy.

Background On McDonald’s Automation

McDonald’s and other fast food companies have been pressured by the “Fight for $15” movement. This movement, in essence, is fighting for the federal minimum wage to be raised to $15 per hour. Currently, the federal minimum wage in the United States is just $7.25 per hour, and it has not been raised since 2009.

The movement started in 2012, and it has now turned into a global movement. Although many states have raised their minimum wage to a number greater than $7.25, the federal minimum wage remains at $7.25.

Obviously, if this “Fight for $15” were to ever be successful and the federal government raised the minimum wage to $15 per hour, it would pose a major threat to companies like McDonald’s. It relies on minimum wage pay to help support its menu of affordable fast food.

A jump in minimum wage from $7.25 to $15 would be a 106% increase, which would more than double McDonald’s labor costs.

The fast food company is already planning for this possibility. McDonald’s has already started automating many of its stores, even in California, where the minimum wage for fast food is $20 per hour.

One of the main things McDonald’s is doing to automate its restaurants is implementing self-serve kiosks. The biggest benefit that these kiosks offer is that they allow the restaurants to take more orders at one time. By some estimates, if automation techniques like kiosks could eliminate 8 employees at a fast food location (like McDonald's), this could help each franchise save up to $326,312 per year. If just ½ this number (so 4 employee roles) were eliminated due to automation, like kiosks (or more of the full service as we have seen from the McDonald's pilot), this would still be $163,156 in annual savings per store. This is a big deal.

“...[an automated kiosk] improves the overall flow of customers within the restaurant and allows McDonald’s to serve a larger number of patrons simultaneously,” the article by Wavetec wrote.

Here’s how this makes McDonald’s corporate more money: each McDonald's store is required to pay roughly 5% of gross sales per year in a royalty to McDonald's corporate (this is a big part of the corporate entity’s revenue). With more automation, each store can serve more food at a lower cost (thereby further increasing foot traffic). It's a win-win for franchisees and for corporate.

In fact, these new menu items (which I believe are partially financially feasible because of automation) are directly listed as a foot traffic driver for the fast food giant.

McDonald’s biggest competitor, Wendy’s, has also been using AI in its drive-thru windows to allow consumers to order their food without talking to an actual human. This is something that McDonald’s experimented with for a brief period of time, but it has since pulled back from AI ordering in drive-thrus (I think they will come back to this later).

McDonald’s has said it’s looking to “evaluate long-term, scalable solutions” for AI ordering in drive-thrus, and it hopes to find a solution by the end of 2024. I’m optimistic about what these automations will bring as well.

Earnings Preview

Even with concerns about the E. Coli outbreak, McDonald’s earnings estimates look favorable over the coming quarters as these new foot traffic. Among 28 analysts, EPS estimates are pegged to come in at $3.20/share and revenue at $6.81 billion for the company. They’re set to report on October 29th before the market opens.

Growth from here is set to accelerate (and I believe this is due in part to automation). EPS is supposed to clock in at $3.44/share by Q4 2026.

In the Q2 Earnings Call, Executive Vice President Ian Borden spoke about how automation has positively impacted the business in every way.

“...our customer satisfaction scores in the US are at kind of an all-time high for this point in the year,” Borden said.

We're seeing that pretty consistently around the world. We're getting faster, we're delivering a better experience. And when you put all that together, that's what kind of defines value for the consumer. And we certainly are adamant and relentless that we're going to get that right in each and every market to be in a winning position -Q2 Call.

While most of these bullish EPS forecasts came before the E. Coli outbreak this past week, I continue to be long run bullish.

On the earnings call, I will be looking for commentary on how automation will be able to help the business even further. I’ll dive into this in the risks section, but I think automation can help solve the E. Coli outbreak/prevent the next one as well.

Valuation

From a traditional valuation standpoint, let's be clear: McDonald’s stock's forward GAAP P/E ratio is 45.77% higher than the sector median number of 18.56 (coming in at 27.06), giving the fast food chain a quant rating of a D+.

Despite this, I think valuing the company on traditional P/E metrics misses the main idea.

As Ray Kroc, a former CEO of McDonald’s from 1961-1984, said, McDonald’s is in the real estate business.

“I’m not in the hamburger business. My business is real estate,” Kroc once told a group of Harvard students. McDonald’s essentially is in this business today as well by charging rent to their franchisees and by collecting a royalty rate of 5%. This is much more stable cash flow.

The whole goal of the McDonald’s business is to make their franchisees more successful so that the real estate that the franchisees put their stores on is worth more. McDonald’s then charges rent, it charges money for food and supplies to be delivered to the store, and they loan the franchisees' money as well.

Given all these things, their cash flow is still far more predictable than a normal food establishment. I think McDonald’s trading at 27.06 times earnings, especially when it’s becoming increasingly automated, is actually very reasonable (if not undervalued).

From here, I strongly believe earnings-per-share growth will accelerate as McDonald’s adopts more automation.

I think we can see McDonald’s trade at closer to 35 times earnings. If shares were to converge on this forward P/E, this would represent 29.34% upside.

E. Coli & Foot Traffic Are Risks, But Automation Can Help Here Too

The main risks for McDonald's center around the overall health concern of US consumers.

What I mean by this is both the actual physical health of consumers, but also their financial health.

Before this E. Coli outbreak, McDonald’s saw slowing foot traffic at many locations over the last 18 months as the prices of McDonald's menu items have outpaced inflation. These new value deals have helped bring customers back in store. But these new E. Coli outbreaks may serve as a temporary setback.

At the same time, other fast food chains have started implementing a value strategy to help support their brands. McDonald’s competitor Subway recently just rolled out a $6.99 offer on any of its foot long subs.

To help compete against other chains (like Subway) and fight off E. Coli fears, McDonald's can further leverage automation to not only further control labor costs, but also improve food quality. New industrial automation systems can actually scan meat for diseases (which would help the brand significantly improve food quality at stores and lower disease risks). Companies like Veritide offer tools to help detect fecal matter on raw meat, lowering the odds that contaminated meat is served at McDonald's restaurants.

Bottom Line

McDonald’s (facing both an E. Coli outbreak and wage pressures from employees) knows it has to adapt to continue to serve iconic food at affordable prices here in the US (and around the globe).

The company has been (and likely will increasingly be) a pioneer in the fast food space with new automation systems. The fast food chain's rollout of new automation tools really allow the company to serve more customers in a shorter amount of time. This leads to more revenue (and more royalties) for McDonald's corporate.

I believe McDonald’s has set itself up in a strong position to where even if the federal minimum wage is raised to $15 per hour, costs increase won’t nearly be as bad for the company. This is because the proliferation of automated tools will help lower overall store level labor costs.

With this, I think McDonald's has a unique opportunity in front of them to accelerate EPS growth. I believe we will start to see the results of this as they go to report earnings this quarter. Despite the E. Coli outbreak, I think shares are a strong buy.

data.title: e.l.f. Beauty Q2 Preview: 45% Upside Ahead As Investor Pessimism Is Overdone
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

I last covered e.l.f. Beauty (NYSE:ELF) on September 1, where I reiterated my “buy” rating based on belief that the company’s robust product innovation, compelling price points, and engaging marketing engine should enable it to gain market share in its Color Cosmetics and Skin Care revenue segments, with plenty of room for expansion in international markets as well as in its Beauty Squad Loyalty program.

However, since the time of my last writing, the stock has declined over 27%, primarily on the back of underwhelming Nielsen POS (point of sales) data that pointed to a weakening demand environment, leading analysts to cut back their estimates for revenue and earnings growth over the next few years.

With the company set to report its Q2 FY25 earnings on 6th November, I believe that the stock is trading at levels where investor pessimism has created an extremely attractive opportunity from a risk-reward perspective.

In this post, I will discuss 3 areas in the earnings report that I will be paying close attention to, to assess its long-term investment thesis.

1. Momentum in Market Share Gains in Color, Skin Care and International are key to watch.

In the previous quarter, e.l.f. Beauty reported revenue growth of 50% YoY to $324.5M, which beat estimates. This was driven by multiple strong performances in all of its revenue segments, which included that its Color Cosmetics segment gained market share by 260 basis points to 12% as revenue grew 26% YoY, while its Skin Care segment continued to outperform with 45% YoY growth, with Naturium driving 16 points to net sales growth.

At the same time, we also saw its International segment continue to grow at a superior rate of 91% YoY, contributing a larger share to Total Revenue with outperformance in its UK and Canada markets, where it partnered with leading beauty retailers to optimally position their brands to attract and convert users.

In my previous post, I had outlined that it was because of e.l.f. Beauty’s superior product innovation at compelling price points coupled with building emotionally resonant marketing content and leveraging insights from their Beauty Squad Loyalty program that were enabling it to maximize average sales per SKU while gaining shelf space at major retailers both in the US and internationally.

Moving forward, e.l.f. Beauty is expected to generate $287.47M in revenue, which would represent a sequential slowdown to 32.8% YoY growth rate. In the meantime, the management had raised its revenue guidance for the full year in its previous earnings report to $1.29B for the full FY25, up from its previous estimate of $1.24B, which would represent a year-over-year growth rate of 26%.

In other words, it was previously expected that the revenue growth rate would slow from its current levels as a result of tougher comps. However, what has soured investor sentiment is that there have been negative revisions to its revenue guidance for FY26 and FY27, as can be seen in the chart below, as a result of underwhelming Nielsen POS data for the four weeks ended August 24, which grew 16% YoY and saw a deceleration of 640 basis points from the four-week period ended July 27.

In its upcoming earnings report, I believe it will be key to watch for management commentary on its market share momentum gains across its Color Cosmetics, Skin Care and International segments while simultaneously driving user acquisition and engagement in its Beauty Squad Loyalty program.

Meanwhile, Raymond James analyst Olivia Tong highlighted that the recent selloff has created a buying opportunity in e.l.f. Beauty after meeting with the management, who had confidence to grow sales and profits, well ahead of peers. At the same time, Piper Sandler analyst Korinne Wolfmeyer also said in her research note that they would “buy on the weakness” as the recent slowdown in demand trends is strictly macro-driven and there doesn’t seem to be any notable share loss to competitors. Finally, Truist analyst Bill Chappell also had a “buy” rating on the stock, as he believes that the scanner issues have largely played out with the stock trading at the lowest level since August 2022.

2. Profit margins may come under pressure from lower volumes and ASPs if the demand environment worsens.

In my previous post, I had discussed that e.l.f. Beauty saw a shrinkage in its Adjusted EBITDA margin from 34% to 24% on a year-over-year basis as SG&A expenses grew, particularly with marketing and digital investment that accounted for 23% of sales, compared to 16% in the previous year. However, the management outlined that it expects adjusted EBITDA growth to accelerate into the back half of FY25 as they cycle the inclusion of Naturium in their financials and see normalized rates of marketing investment on a year-over-year basis.

For Q3, e.l.f. Beauty is projected to generate EPS (earnings per share) of $0.44, which represents a year-over-year decrease of 46%, after which earnings estimates are expected to rise with improving comps, as can be seen in the chart below. Plus, for the full year FY25, the management had also raised its guidance for Adjusted EBITDA to approximately $299M, up from $287M, which would represent a margin of 23%.

However, similar to revenue growth, its earnings growth projections have been revised downwards for the next 3 years, on the back of weaker demand projections from the POS data and general macroeconomic uncertainty. Unfortunately, in this event, margins will likely suffer in the short term, weighing down on investor sentiment. Therefore, it will be important to pay attention to management commentary on how they are navigating the current environment of slowing demand to ensure that they can sustain their profit margins while driving targeted investments to gain market share across revenue segments and geographies.

3. Inventory levels to be monitored in order to sustain strength in the free cash position.

Finally, investors need to pay attention to its inventory level, which had grown from $98M to $200M in the previous quarter on a year-over-year basis. While the management had noted that the buildup of inventory was tied to supporting strong consumer demand, along with Naturium adding $26M of inventory in the previous quarter, a weakening demand environment could worsen its working capital position and put downward pressure on its free cash flow.

Tying it together: At least 45% upside ahead.

Taking the management’s FY25 projections and the latest consensus estimates for revenue and earnings for the next two years into account, I believe e.l.f. Beauty should generate close to $1.75B in revenue after growth slows in the high to mid-teens after FY25. I believe that this will be achievable given e.l.f. Beauty’s commitment to driving robust product innovation at compelling price points, coupled with a go-to-market strategy that is centered around building marketing moments to attract and convert users, leading to higher market share in its Color Cosmetics, Skin Care segments along with expanding internationally.

Meanwhile, from a profitability standpoint, taking the consensus estimates for non-GAAP EPS of $5.29 in FY27, we can see that it will be growing at a faster rate than overall revenue growth, which, I believe, will be made possible as the company is able to improve its unit economics through higher purchase volume and order value from its customers, especially as it grows the size of its user base on its Beauty Squad Loyalty program. This will be equivalent to a present value of $4.54 when discounted at 8%.

Taking the S&P 500 as a proxy, where its companies increase their earnings on average by 8% over a 10-year period, with a price-to-earnings ratio of 15-18, I believe that e.l.f. Beauty should trade at least two times the multiple, given the growth rate of its earnings during this period of time. This will translate to a PE ratio of 34, or a price target of $154, which represents an upside of 45% from its current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

I believe that the 27% decline in the stock price since my last writing has created a unique investment opportunity for the stock. While investors have rightfully gotten spooked after the underwhelming POS data that led to downward revisions in both the company’s future revenue and earnings growth targets, I believe the magnitude of the pessimism is overdone at this point. As an investor and a user of the brand, I will be carefully watching for the company’s progress in gaining market share in its Color Cosmetics and Skin Care segments, while it simultaneously expands internationally by partnering with leading beauty retailers. Although its profit margins and inventory levels might see some short-term pressure, I believe that the company’s product innovation, go-to-market strategies, and loyalty program should help it continue to grow its revenue in at least the mid- to high teens range over the next couple of years, with an upside of at least 45% in the stock price from its current levels.

data.title: Washington Trust: The New Macroeconomic Environment Could Be Favorable (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Washington Trust Bancorp, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:WASH) Q3 2024 was recently released, and the market reacted positively with +8.06%. Underlying this optimism is more conservative balance sheet management as well as a return to growth in non-interest-bearing deposits. In addition, both EPS and revenue estimates were beaten.

In my previous article, I highlighted my doubts about the sustainability of the dividend, but after this quarterly report, I am slightly more optimistic thanks to the reduction in the Loan-to-Deposit ratio. In Q2 2024 this ratio had reached 113%, while today it has dropped to 106%; it is still high, but at least there are improvements.

Highlight Q3 2024

For the loan-to-deposit ratio to fall, it was necessary for the bank to curb the increase in loans on the balance sheet, and in fact, it did.

Total loans reached $5.51 billion, down 2% from the previous quarter and 1.70% from last year. In particular, the decline in the last three months can be attributed mainly to commercial loans, down 3%. This does not mean that there were no new loans, but that pay-downs were greater. However, an increase in underwriting is expected starting in the next quarter, which is why a simultaneous increase in deposits is needed to avoid wasting the improvements made on the loan-to-deposit ratio.

I think coming into this year, I think we told you we were going to pull back a bit on our origination activity. So that's kind of being reflected in the numbers you see, it's normal pay-downs. And we also managed out a few credits intentionally. But we think that that's behind us now and we're expecting to start to ramp up underwriting in the fourth quarter.

Ned Handy - Chairman, Chief Executive Officer, conference call Q3 2024.

Do not expect double-digit growth for commercial loans, it is expected around 1-2%, at least until Q1 2025. From that point on, the situation should improve.

Management probably expects this rebound since they are confident about deposit growth.

After an underwhelming period, total deposits in the last quarter returned to growth and reached $5.17 billion, up 3.90% from the previous quarter. However, what was most important was the boost in non-interest-bearing deposits, +$20 million compared to Q2 2024 and +$16 million compared to Q1 2024.

The Fed's change in monetary policy is giving WASH deposits oxygen and the bottom of non-interest-bearing deposits may have been touched last quarter.

But I can tell you that on the way down, those deposit betas will be a lot higher than they were on the way up. We have been aggressively looking at our money market deposits and keeping an eye on the competition and being mindful of customer retention. I think we're doing a good job of repricing those money markets down, but there is still a little bit of a lag compared to where we are on the loan. So that will probably impact us a bit, but we view that as timing. Our balance sheet is liability-sensitive, and the rate reductions will definitely help us.

Ron Ohsberg - Chief Financial Officer, conference call Q3 2024.

WASH has a liability-sensitive balance sheet, so this means that its liabilities reprice faster than assets. In a lower interest rate environment, this is the ideal scenario because the cost of deposits falls faster than the yield on assets, resulting in a higher net interest margin. In any case, at least in the first moments of expansionary monetary policy, we will not notice these improvements because of a temporary timing problem.

In fact, the net interest margin in Q4 2024 should be the same as it is now, 1.85%. Starting in 2025 the situation will shift, and if the Fed continues to reduce interest rates WASH will improve in terms of profitability. This is not an immediate process, and consider that with each cut there will be a time lag before the bank can benefit. So, it is challenging to give accurate estimates, but we know that during a rate cutting cycle, WASH is well-positioned.

Returning to the issue of loans, it is worth noting that their reduction in the balance sheet was a company choice, not dictated by market conditions. In fact, the demand for loans experienced by WASH was quite good, better than in previous quarters.

In order to take advantage of the demand without increasing the loan-to-deposit ratio, WASH focused mainly on originating new loans to sell in the secondary market; that is why total mortgage banking revenues ($2.86 million) increased by 36% compared to Q3 2023.

Taking a look at total mortgage loan originations, you can see a quarter-on-quarter progression, a sign that demand for credit is picking up again after the recent drop in interest rates: mortgage originations have not been this high since Q3 2023. Of the current $141.43 million, 85% has been sold, but once deposits return to growth consistently, an increasing amount will flow back into the balance sheet.

Finally, let's briefly take a look at the dividend given the yield is very high at 6.50%.

I have already done several more detailed articles on its sustainability, especially during a recession, but now we focus on the short term.

WASH has issued a dividend every quarter for 30 years in a row, but has not always been able to increase it. When it is in distress it tends to leave it constant until it has the capital needed to increase it again.

Currently, the dividend payout ratio is quite high, 83.90%, suggesting that we are in one of those difficult phases for WASH. However, taking advantage of its liability-sensitive balance sheet, as interest rates fall, earnings should return to growth, making the dividend more sustainable. For this reason, I am quite optimistic that it will at least manage not to decrease it.

Much will depend on how many cuts the Fed makes, as a macroeconomic environment with high rates is not helping this bank. Obviously, too many cuts in 2025 is not a desirable scenario because it would mean facing an imminent recession. In a sense, the ideal scenario for WASH is a Fed that gradually cuts rates while the economy remains strong.

Conclusion

WASH is a bank that has suffered greatly from rising interest rates, and its profitability has declined rapidly. Deposits have become too expensive, and loan growth has been slowed by a high loan-to-deposit ratio. In Q3 2024 there were signs of encouragement from this standpoint, in fact, it fell to 106%.

We cannot expect a marked improvement as early as next quarter in terms of net interest margin, but by mid-2025 this could change, which is why the market rewarded WASH with a jump of 8.03%. The structure of its balance sheet is more biased toward a falling rate environment, and this factor may make the dividend more sustainable. The U.S. economy is showing strong resilience despite everything, so at least for the moment, a recession is not on the radar. In addition, demand for credit is robust as evidenced by the increase in mortgage loan originations.

The price per share has risen a lot in the past six months (33.83%), but there may still be room for further upside.

The Price/TBV per share is 1.35x, still very low compared to the 10-year average of 2.05x. Multiplying the latter figure by the current TBV per share, the fair value would amount to $52.29, well above the current price. Thus, considering the improved business sustainability, coupled with WASH's potential in a different macroeconomic environment, I believe it deserves an upgrade from hold to buy.

data.title: Here's How Not To Invest $100,000
data.description:
Risk comes from not knowing what you're doing - Warren Buffett

Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction

There are 101 new mistakes that investors can make when investing. I'd like to say that only newer investors make these mistakes, but the truth is - even more experienced investors can make them.

And like Warren Buffett's timeless wisdom reminds us: Risk comes from not knowing what you're doing.

Some people can jump off a 40m cliff with their hands spread and belly first, a practice called death jumping. They hit the water, and resurface a few seconds later, pumping their fists and high faving their friends.

Of course, there is a method to the madness. If you or I were to blindly try this tomorrow, there would be risks.

So two people can do the same thing, and one person can do it with low risk and the other can do it with high risk.

When it comes to investing, you likely won't break your neck if you get it wrong, but your spouse might if you make big enough mistakes often enough.

It's a good idea to wise up and avoid common mistakes. More than mistakes, there are blunders.

In chess, we categorize moves as "blunders," when they lead to a significant deterioration of position, exposing you to a checkmate or giving up a strategic advantage.

When you've got cash to invest, you've got a strategic advantage. Make bad decisions, and you're going to blunder it away.

If you have $100,000 to invest in stocks, that's a nice little nest egg to nurture and grow.

In this article, I will expose five blunders which investors commonly make, and which you want to avoid if you don't want to be wiping a zero off that $100K of yours.

I will give examples, some mine, some of the others, so that you can learn. If you can learn from others, you learn for free. If you learn from experience, you pay the full price.

Blunder 1: Blindly chasing high yields.

The temptation to chase abnormally high yields can come to many newer investors who forget the most important rule of economic theory, "there's no free lunch." Like all rules, there are exceptions, but for the most part, a high yield reflects one of the following:

  • Significant risk to revenue growth, or even risk of declines.
  • Significant risk to profitability, maybe through debt exposure.
  • Which can lead to erosion in the value of assets, or dividend cuts.

A great example of this is Eagle Point Credit (ECC). It's not a stock per se, but a high yield closed end fund which invests in CLOs.

ECC might seem enticing as it yields 17%. A quick look at the DFT chart below, however shows that over the past decade, the share price has gone from $20 to under $10.

What's the point of a high yield if the value of your capital is going to be severely eroded over time.

Unsurprisingly, the share price of this closed end fund has been tightly correlated to the underlying net asset value, or NAV. The NAV has been cut in half in the past decade.

Why? Because over time, ECC pays out more in dividends than it earns from the vehicles in which it invests.

It basically lures in unknowing investors by giving them a return of their investment rather than a return on their investment.

Eroding value of your capital is maybe how socialists would want to run a business, but it should not be your approach. Remember more of Buffett's wise words:

The first rule of an investment is don’t lose money. The second rule of an investment is don’t forget the first rule.

Yet, I've read many articles over time touting of ECC's virtue. The main point is its yield. I won't point fingers, but you can find analysts that lauded ECC when it yielded 14% in 2019, and kept doubling down. "ECC now yields 16%". "Wow, can you believe it? A 17% yield."

You need to wise up. Chasing yield is a bad idea.

Does that mean you shouldn't chase stocks or securities with high yields? Not necessarily.

I'm not automatically opposed to high-yielding stocks. We just we need to approach them with caution and understand why they have a high yield.

For instance, we chose to invest in Arbor Realty (ABR) in March 2023 after a bogus short report was published, which we debunked.

At the time, Arbor shares were trading at $11.5 and the stock yielded 13.9%. Today shares are at $15.25, and including the big dividend, Arbor has returned a total of 64% vs. the S&P 500's 49.39%.

Another example is ONEOK (OKE). We bought shares in May 2020 when the stock was yielding 12% and investors feared the dividend would be cut. We understood the risk, but believed in management's vision to maintain the dividend throughout the pandemic.

The stock has returned a total of 300% since then, vs. the S&P 500's 100% return.

So it should be clear that the key word in blunder No. 1 is blindly chasing high yields. If you know what you're doing, it can pay off, but there are risks which you need to understand and master, if you don't want to be holding on to assets which just keep losing value.

Blunder 2: Giving dividend history too much importance.

I blame this blunder on the prominence of Dividend Aristocrat indices and Dividend Champions lists.

The names "champions" and "aristocrats" make these stocks seem like they are untouchable. As if their history of paying outgrowing dividends for many years and even decades gave them an absolute sense of safety.

But I spent most of my childhood in France. And we were taught in history class, that often, it doesn't end too well for aristocrats.

Just this year, four of these well-known safe blue chips cut their dividends. I've owned all of these four at some point, which goes to show I've been guilty of this mistake quite a few times.

VF Corp. (VFC) cut its dividend for the second time in December 2023, reducing the payout for 2024 again. This was one of our worst investment decisions of the past years.

Fool me once, blame on your, fool me twice, blame on me. Don't wait for the second dividend cut.

Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA): Announced a 48% dividend cut on Jan. 4, 2024, reducing the quarterly dividend to $0.25. Decades of dividend growth undermined by a big cut. This was a mistake of ours. But we bailed right after the big cut, which meant we exited around $20 a stock, which now trades at $10. We'll talk about this more later.

Intel (INTC): Had already announced a 66% dividend cut in 2023. In August, they cut the dividend altogether. That's more than 30 years of diligent dividend increases gone. We bailed after the first dividend cut, which was first viewed as a bad move and is now clear it was a good one.

3M (MMM): Announced a 54% dividend cut in May 2024.

When we sold our shares of 3M at $200 in 2021, just a year after having bought at $140 in 2020, some of our readers were confused.

We saw the writing on the wall for the dividend, with the very small dividend increases being symptomatic of the legal woes the company was going through. This culminated in a dividend cut.

Sometimes we get it right, sometimes we don't. Live and learn. Be better than last time.

Blunder 3: Holding on to your losers

As I hope it is clear from blunder No. 2, everyone is going to make some mistakes sometimes. But the best you can do, is to recognize these, take responsibility, take the L, and move on.

Holding on to losers can cost a lot.

Case in point CVS Health (CVS), which we had initiated coverage on in April 2023, but decided by June of this year to exit. I explained in a memo to members of the Dividend Freedom Tribe:

We got into CVS last year based on giving management's integration plan more weight than it deserved. Based on this idea of integration, we believed the firm could consolidate an interesting moat in its industry.

From $73 in April 2023 when we initiated coverage, CVS has declined to $61, declining 17%

Management recently took part in Bernstein's annual strategic decisions conference, where management of various companies are interviewed on the strategic outlook of their firm.

Rather than giving me more confidence, this conference shattered it.

Management was cagey, showing signs of oversight, missing the ball and not offering a way to overcome their challenges.

Since then, the stock has declined further to $57, giving up another 6%.

Now of course a 6% loss isn't much, but consider that around the same time we were saying "let's bail on CVS" we were saying that Omega Healthcare (OHI), Morgan Stanley (MS), Manulife Financial (MFC), Philip Morris (PM) and Inditex (OTCPK:IDEXY) were top buys for H2 2024.

All of those picks are up by at least 20% since then, as you can see on the chart, while CVS is down another 6%.

Holding on to a loser is a bad idea, because if the fundamentals have changed, it will likely go down more, and you're giving up on the returns you could get from your actual good ideas. The chart above should be a reminder of that. As Peter Lynch reminds us in his book One up on Wall Street:

Just because a stock goes down doesn't mean it can't go lower

Blunder 4: Failing to diversify

In the investing world, whatever Warren Buffett has said is often taken as gospel. Buffett is known for having said:

Diversification is protection against ignorance. It makes little sense if you know what you are doing.

This should not be your ticket to building a five-stock portfolio and calling it a day.

Peter Lynch said we should diversify because:

You should invest in several stocks because out of every five you pick one will be very great, one will be really bad, and three will be OK

And if you're starting out, you might expect 1 to be great, two to be bad, and two to be OK.

If you only buy five stocks, you might be buying five really bad ones. If you buy 20 or 30 or even 40, I'm pretty sure you'll have some winners.

You'll be able to cut the losers, double down on the winners when it makes sense, and come out on top.

Our portfolios have done extremely well by being extremely diversified. Diversification is the only free lunch in investing. You should make the most of it.

Let's take some of Buffett's advice and admit that there are some unknowns in the world and the economy which we are ignorant about. We don't ALWAYS know what we're doing, even if we think we do.

Blunder 5: Being too cheap for your own good.

In the DIY investing community, just like in the DIY building community (Robert is very big on DIY) people are naturally thrifty.

And being thrifty is good. In investing, like in life, if you focus on getting good deals, then you'll come out on top.

But for many people, there seems to be a blurred line between thriftiness and being outright cheap.

Yes, you can save a lot of money by doing a construction project yourself, but you should still likely pay an electrician and a plumber to do their bit, which requires a lot of expertise.

In the same way, in investing you should likely stay away from expensive mutual funds which take money on the way in, on the way out, and while you stay with them. These fees will eat into your performance like crazy.

But you must also be realistic: How much time do you really have to building and managing a diversified portfolio. If you're going to pick the stocks yourself, because you believe that an index doesn't cater to your needs, and that mutual funds are too expensive, then what parts of the process should you outsource to others?

Are you really going to read quarterly reports for 30, 40, 50 or even 100 companies, and allocate time to analyze, assess, and update your position on this stock?

If the answer is no, maybe you should outsource at least part of the research to those you trust and do your own due diligence. If that means giving up a few dollars in subscription fees, it might be worth it.

Conclusion

Warren Buffett's late business partner Charlie Munger liked to quote Carl Gustav Jacob Jacobi, who said:

Invert, always invert.

Sometimes, to determine the way one should do something, it's best to first consider how one would ensure failure, as doing the opposite might ensure success.

If you don't chase yield blindly, you don't use dividend history as a be-all end-all for dividend safety and quality, you cut your losers in time, you build a diversified portfolio, and you're realistic about how much time you can allocate to investing, then maybe being an active investor is a good idea for you.

Otherwise, investing that $100,000 yourself might not be the best plan.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ionis Pharmaceuticals: Catalysts Due In 2025/2026 Are Extremely Compelling
data.description:
Investment Overview

I have been considering Ionis (NASDAQ:IONS) as a potential "strong buy" opportunity for some time. I covered the Carlsbad, California-based developer of antisense, oligonucleotide therapeutics on multiple occasions, most recently in May, shortly after management released Q1 earnings.

I reiterated my "Buy" recommendation, albeit downgrading from "Strong Buy", to 'Buy". Let's briefly recap on the company's approach to drug development, as per its 2023 annual report/10K submission:

Antisense medicines target RNA, the intermediary that conveys genetic information from a gene to the protein synthesis machinery in the cell. By targeting RNA instead of proteins, we can use antisense technology to increase, decrease or alter the production of specific proteins. Most of our antisense medicines are designed to bind to mRNAs and inhibit the production of disease-causing proteins.

We also now use small interfering RNA (siRNA) technology, in addition to antisense technology, in the development of new medicines. Like antisense, siRNA medicines target RNA, and can decrease the production of specific proteins involved in disease.

Our advanced LICA technology is a chemical technology we developed that involves attaching a molecule called a ligand that binds with receptors on the surface of cells in a highly specific manner. Because these receptors are often found only on certain cell types, LICA allows us to increase effective delivery of our antisense medicines with higher specificity to certain cell types that express these receptors relative to non-conjugated antisense medicines.

This approach has enabled Ionis to secure five commercial approvals for drug candidates - SPINRAZA (nusinersen), in spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) QALSODY (tofersen), in superoxide dismutase 1 amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (SOD1-ALS), WAINUA (eplontersen), in polyneuropathy of hereditary transthyretin-mediated amyloidosis (ATTRv-PN), TEGSEDI (inotersen), also for ATTRv-PN and WAYLIVRA (volanesorsen), for familial chylomicronemia syndrome (FCS).

Recent Performance - Q2 Earnings Overview

Five drug approvals within a highly promising, albeit restrictive (mainly due to the difficulty of delivering antisense/siRNA drugs to the desired target cells) field of drug development is an impressive feat, however Ionis is no commercial powerhouse.

As we can see above, Ionis' 1H24 and 2Q24 income statements - shared in its Q2 earnings press release - show the company earned $225m of revenues in Q2, and $345m in 1H, up respectively ~20% and 8%.

The issues weighing on the company valuation (~$6bn at the time of writing) as ever with Ionis, are meagre product revenues, and heavy losses: a $(209m) net loss was recorded in 1H24, versus a $(210)m loss in the prior year period. As mentioned in my last note, annual losses amount to $(386m) in 2023, $(270m) in 2022, $(29m) in 2021, and $(444m) in 2020.

Guidance for full-year 2024 remained unchanged after Q2 updates, for revenues of ~$575m, losses of "less than" $(475m), and an end of year cash balance of ~$1.7bn. As of Q2, $2.1bn of cash was reported.

In summary, then, revenues in the second half of 2024 are going to be lower than in the first half, and the company will keep losing money, which is not usually a recipe for share price growth.

The real strength and growth potential of Ionis lies in its late-stage pipeline, however, and its potentially lucrative partnerships with Big Pharma companies. Ionis says it has >$23bn of potential milestone payments in play, while as we can see below (slide taken from a recent investor presentation), the late-stage pipeline includes 9 medicines in Phase 3 studies, targeting eleven separate indications:

Partnerships and Pipeline - Biogen, AstraZeneca, & The Promise Of Wainua

Commercial revenues accounted for only $132m of the $345m earned in 1H24, the vast majority of that earned via Spinraza royalties.

Spinraza is marketed and sold by Ionis' partner Biogen (BIIB), a company that has been working collaboratively with Ionis since 2013, and has paid Ionis >$3.9bn via milestone payments and payments to purchase stock (according to Ionis' Q2 quarterly report/10Q submission) during that period. Qalsody is wholly owned by Biogen with Ionis not eligible for royalties on sales, however, in 1H24 Ionis reported it earned $197m of revenues from Biogen, versus $167m in the prior year period.

Anglo/Swedish Pharma giant AstraZeneca is responsible for marketing and selling Wainua - according to Ionis's latest 10Q:

Over the term of our WAINUA collaboration, we are eligible to receive up to $3.6 billion, which is comprised of a $200 million upfront payment, up to $485 million in development and approval milestone payments and up to $2.9 billion in sales milestone payments.

In addition, we are eligible to receive up to mid-20 percent royalties for sales in the U.S. and tiered royalties up to the high teens for sales outside the U.S.

AstraZeneca reported $21m of Wainua revenues in 1H24, but the drug only secured approval in the US in December 2023, and in Canada in Q2, so sales will pick up momentum, especially if an approval in Europe is secured, which is very likely, and most importantly, if a follow-on approval in ATTR cardiomyopathy - a 500k patient population - can be secured.

Ionis is running a Phase 3 study in ATTR-CM - a leading cause of heart failure - which is now fully enrolled, with 1.4k patients. The primary endpoint is in cardiovascular (CV) mortality and hospitalization, while CV mortality as well as closed mortality are secondary endpoints. Data from the study is expected in 2026.

In my view, the outcome of this study is critical for Ionis - this is a multi-billion dollar indication and analysts have speculated the drug could achieve $1 - $2bn of revenues per annum if approval is achieved, which implies a $375m per annum royalty payment, plus another $500m in approval milestone payments, and another $2.9bn in sales milestone across the products life-cycle. In summary, approval in ATTR-CM could transform Wainua from a $100m per annum revenue product (for Ionis) at best, to a $500m per annum product for the company.

As a once-monthly drug that is injectable at home, the marketability of the drug should be strong, and the fact that AstraZeneca will be doing the marketing and selling is another positive.

Competition comes from Pfizer's oral product Vyndaqel/Vyndamax - $3.3bn of revenues in 2023, and $1.3bn in Q124 alone - and, subject to approval, BridgeBio's (BBIO) acoramidis, Alnylam's (ALNY) Amvuttra, and further candidates in development from AstraZeneca itself, and Danish Pharma giant Novo Nordisk (NVO).

When Alnylam shared positive Phase data from its Phase study of amvuttra in ATTR-CM in June, its share price leaped from ~$155 per share to >$250 per share, a gain of >60%. Alnylam is now pushing for approval and will likely be first to market, but Ionis/AstraZeneca, whose drug has a similar mechanism of action, based around RNA-interference as opposed to e.g. protein stabilisation, which is what Vyndaqel does, has every chance of following in Alnylam footsteps and entering the market in 2027, I'd speculate, around two years after Amvuttra.

Olezarsen, Pelacarsen, Bepiroversen

Meanwhile Olezarsen looks set to become, according to Ionis' CEO, Brett Monia (quoted on the Q2 call with analysts) "our first independent launch of a wholly owned medicine for Ionis", with a Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) date (when the FDA announces whether a drug is accepted or rejected for approval) of December 19th.

The indication is FCS, however, which will provide very little in the way of meaningful revenues - the much larger indication here is severe hypertriglyceridemia (SHTG), and Ionis has three fully enrolled pivotal/Phase 3 studies in ~2.4k patients altogether in patients with SHTG< with data from all three studies promised in H225.

The main rival in this space is Arrowhead's (ARWR) plozasiran, which is also chasing approval in FCS and expected to enter Phase 3 studies soon. With no approvals secured in its history, however, and less funding available, Ionis looks to be the frontrunner here, supported by data from mid-stage studies demonstrating "robust reductions in ApoC-III and triglycerides at 6 and 12 months."

Ionis/Arrowhead's hope is that triglyceride targeting drugs become the new statins (as I discussed in a recent note of ARWR) which would open up a "blockbuster" revenue opportunity for Olezarsen. Given Ionis would keep all revenues from sales of the drug, another self-injectable, this is arguably as important an opportunity as Wainua.

The other two major markets Ionis is targeting - this time with partners - are cardiovascular disease ("CVD"), through Pelacarsen, partnered with Swiss Pharma Novartis (NVS), and hepatitis B, through bepirovirsen, partnered with GSK.

For bepirovirsen, a Phase 3 study is underway, and fully enrolled, with data due in 2026, and according to Ionis' latest 10Q:

GSK reported positive results from Phase 2 studies in 2023, including durable response data from the Phase 2 B-Sure long-term follow-up study of bepirovirsen in complete responder patients from the Phase 2b B-Clear study of patients with HBV. In February 2024, the FDA granted Fast Track designation to bepirovirsen for the treatment of patients with HBV.

GSK believes this could be a >$2bn per annum selling product, and Ionis says "we are eligible to receive tiered royalties up to the low-teens on net sales of bepirovirsen", as well as milestones.

For pelacarsen, I noted in May that "the therapy treats elevated lipoprotein, and could secure approval to treat multiple cardiovascular diseases, taking on the likes of Amgen Inc. (AMGN)/Alnylam's olpasiran" - which is also in Phase 3 studies in CVD. CEO Monie had the following to say about pelacarsen on the Q2 earnings call:

- the only news on Pelacarsen is really that the study is on track to read out as scheduled next year. Everything is going very well. And our partner, Novartis, is well positioned with first mover advantage in this first-to-market patient population has enormous patient population. Everything we're seeing in the Phase 3 study is very supportive. This being set up for a successful outcome. First mover advantage is really important, and we with Novartis have substantial lead to the market.

Looking Ahead - Patience Is Key To Unlocking Value

What Ionis has been able to do historically is to develop an innovative technology platform focused on an alternative form of RNA-interference, in antisense oligonucleotides, establish proof-of-concept for numerous candidates targeting a range of diseases, and successfully navigate the clinical study and regulatory approval process to bring several drugs to market.

That is worthy of significant praise, however, the lion's share of revenues for Spinraza, the only drug approved to date generating meaningful revenues (>$2bn per annum) go to partner Biogen, while Ionis' other drugs generated <$40m in 1H24.

As such, the next goal for Ionis is to win approval in larger indications - both for itself and for its many partners - and begin to generate revenues that turn the company from an innovative biotech into a commercial powerhouse.

It has to be said that many of Ionis' pipeline prospects have progressed a little slower than initially expected, with several key data readouts due in 2024 or 2025 being pushed back into the following year, but I don't view this as too much of a concern. Studies are mostly fully enrolled, and the data will arrive sooner or later.

The next question is whether the data will be positive, sufficient to make commercial approval likely? Based on my research, and the known qualities of the antisense / RNA gene silencing drugs already approved, which are safe and effective, I'd say these drugs do have a good chance of making it to market, and are arguably more derisked than most, as they have a similar mechanism of action, not to mention a convenient self-injecting at home administration.

The final question is whether Ionis' drugs will be undermined by superior candidates from the likes of Alnylam, Arrowhead, or Pharma giants like Novo Nordisk?

I'd argue that despite some setbacks on timing, Ionis' partners have generally excellent commercial infrastructure, which is likely to make Ionis' partnered product highly competitive in the marketplace, while Ionis itself is rich enough to fund a successful commercial launch for wholly owned Olezarsen, for example, or donidalorsen, in hereditary angioedema - a potential $500m per annum opportunity, or Zilganersen, in Alexander Disease.

Concluding Thoughts - Nothing To Suggest My 'Buy' Thesis Is In Jeopardy, But Major Catalysts Won't Arrive Until 2025/26

Each time I re-evaluate the investment thesis around Ionis it takes some time to unravel the complex web of milestone payments, disease indications, approval timelines, clinical evidence, and market opportunities.

I suspect that is why Ionis may not be hugely popular with retail investors - a company working on a cancer therapy, or an asthma drug, for example is easier to comprehend than a company using "antisense oligonucleotide" drugs to treat "polyneuropathy of hereditary transthyretin-mediated amyloidosis". It does not exactly trip off the tongue, and frankly, completing the due diligence for Ionis is somewhat exhausting.

It is well worth it, however, in my view, because by 2027 Ionis has a good chance of being worth twice as much as it today. Current market cap is ~$6bn, but if we total up the potential revenues streams ($500m per annum from Wainua, $500m from olezarsen, pelacarsen, and bepirovirsen, $500m from donidalorsen, ~$200m from zilganersen), even conservative estimates suggest Ionis could become a >$3bn per annum revenue company by 2030, and typically such companies have market cap valuations in the double-digit billions. Consider Alnylam, for example, currently worth >$35bn, despite delivering net income of $(442m) in 2023, and revenues of $1.8bn.

My research suggests Ionis' clinical studies have been painstaking, and, while several failures must be acknowledged, many have been successful and unlocked approval shots. This article only considers late-stage pipeline opportunities - but, for example, there is a lot of excitement around the company's Angelman syndrome drug candidate ION582, after positive mid-stage study results. A Pivotal study may begin next year.

At first glance, Ionis, with its relatively weak commercial revenues and heavy losses, looks unattractive as an investment opportunity, but in reality, the company is now moving some potential multi-billion dollar peak revenue drugs towards commercial approval, having already established proof-of-concept with five full FDA approvals.

I don't see any major gains arriving for Ionis stock until next year as the forecast for 2024 earnings is modest, as discussed, but if we consider the fact that Alnylam's positive amvuttra data added close to $10bn to Alnylam's market cap overnight, and Ionis/AstraZeneca may have similarly impactful data to share in 2026, there has to be optimism that Ionis's share price is trading at an attractive discount to its future value.

This is just one of many examples of where Ionis may be able to generate billions of dollars in revenue, and therefore, with the caveat that patience is required, I have little hesitation in awarding Ionis stock another "Buy" rating.

data.title: Alexandria Real Estate: Q3 Results Reinforce Bullish View
data.description:
I last looked at Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (NYSE:ARE) at the beginning of July. At that time, I thought the stock was an attractive investment opportunity because the valuation was well below what it typically traded with.

Even a subtle expansion in the multiple, coupled with the above-average dividend yield, could lead to double-digit total returns in my estimate.

Shares of Alexandria have gained only 1% since my article on the name was published, but my investment thesis remains very much intact. Let’s look at why I continue to believe that Alexandria is a strong buy today.

Takeaways from Alexandria Real Estate's Q3 Earnings Results

Alexandria reported third quarter earnings results on October 21st, 2024.

The company showed strength throughout its business. Revenue grew almost 11% to $791.6 million and was more than $11 million higher than expected. Funds from operations, or FFO, of $2.37 matched expectations, but were 1 cent higher on a sequential basis and compared favorably to $2.26 in the prior year.

Management provided updated guidance as well, with Alexandria expecting adjusted FFO in a range of $9.45 to $9.49, which is a tightening from prior guidance of $9.41 to $9.53. At the midpoint, this would be a 5.5% improvement from the prior year.

Growth on both the top and bottom lines and solid guidance has long been the story for the company. And Alexandria enjoys several trends that should continue to provide tailwinds to its business.

First, the company has a very high occupancy rate of 94.7%, which is a 10 basis point improvement sequentially and a 100 basis point increase year-over-year.

Part of this increase is thanks to the company’s focus on mega campuses. Mega campuses are those properties that are strategically located in clusters around major U.S. cities, such as Boston, New York City, San Diego, San Francisco, and Seattle, that bring together research, development, and testing.

These sites are critical to the healthcare sector as they bring together different biotechnology names for the purpose of finding new treatments for ailments. These sites are some of the most attractive places to work for those in the field, meaning that Alexandria’s properties will likely continue to be staffed.

This is vitally important as 76% of the company’s third quarter rental revenue comes from mega campuses, which is up from 74% in Q2 2024.

Alexandria’s leasing volume was especially high during the quarter. The company leased 1.5 million rentable square feet during the period. For context, the company had an average leasing volume of just 1 million square feet in the prior four quarters. Lease renewals grew 5.1%, showing that the company’s properties are very much in demand and clients are willing to pay up for them.

Alexandria also benefits from having more than half of its annual rental rates coming from investment-grade or publicly traded large tenants.

These factors go a long way in leading the company to a rental collection of 99.9% for the third quarter.

Alexandria should continue to see high rates of occupancy and rental collection, as the weighted average remaining lease term is 7.5 years for the portfolio. Looking at just the top 20 tenants, this figure expands to 9.5 years.

Another area I highlighted in my previous article was the strength of Alexandria’s balance sheet. Long-term debt did tick higher by 2.2% from Q2 2024, but the maturity schedule is spread out. Approximately a fifth of total debt matures in the next five years, including no maturities within the next year.

The weighted average remaining term is 12.6 years and a third of debt does not mature until after 2049, with an average interest rate of below 4%. Not only does the company’s debt maturity spread out over a very long period of time, but almost 98% of its debt is fixed rated, meaning Alexandria has significant visibility regarding how much its debt will cost to finance. Additionally, the company has liquidity of $5.4 billion.

In total, Alexandria has attractive properties located in key areas, collects nearly all its rent on time, maintains a fortress balance sheet, and has long-term contracts, most of which have annual rent escalators. In short, there is much to like about the company’s business model.

ARE Stock Valuation Analysis and Total Return Potential

Analysts are expecting the company to produce FFO near its guidance.

If so, then FFO growth would be nearly 6% from the prior year. This is lower than the five- and 10-year compound annual growth rates of 6.4% and 7.2%.

With the factors working in the company’s favor, I believe that 6% FFO growth, which is below the averages over the medium- and long-term, is sustainable.

Currently, Alexandria is trading at 12.5 times the expected FFO for the year. For much of the last decade, shares have mostly traded within a high-teens to mid-20s multiple. By this measure, shares are vastly underrated relative to its historical performance.

Part of the reason the valuation is much lower than usual is that higher interest rates have weighed on the REIT sector. The cost of issuing debt has been higher recently than it was during much of the previous decade.

Alexandria’s level of debt has expanded, but most of this debt was issued at fixed rates.

Furthermore, the Federal Reserve cut rates last month and the market seems to anticipate further cuts as well. While interest rates will likely never be as low as they were following the 2007 to 2009 financial crisis absent a major market issue, rates may have peaked and further cuts could occur as the economic data remains positive.

Lower rates would be good for the companies in the REIT sector and could provide additional expansion to the multiple, especially once the market starts to pay more attention to Alexandria’s fundamentals.

I reiterate my prior valuation range of 14 to 16 times FFO for the stock, which is still a discount to Alexandria’s long-term range, but incorporates a benefit from lower rates and a well-performing business.

Moving from the current valuation to this range would add a few additional percentage points to annual returns.

The stock’s dividend also deserves special mention. Shares are yielding 4.3% currently, which is not particularly impressive compared to the rest of the REIT sector. However, the current yield is more than 100 basis points higher than Alexandria’s 10-year average yield of 3.2%. Current shareholders are being paid to hold the stock at a much higher rate and at a much better valuation than it typically has over the last decade.

The dividend has a solid growth history as well, with both the medium- and long-term raises at just under 6% per year. And with an expected payout ratio of 55% for 2024, the dividend appears well covered. Future increases are also likely, which should help Alexandria’s 14-year dividend growth streak continue to grow.

My expectation for FFO growth of 6%, the dividend yield, and a benefit from an expanding multiple should combine for at least double-digit growth on an annual basis. And this is my base case. If the multiple reverts even closer to its long-term range, then total returns would be considerably higher.

Risks to Investment Thesis

The risks that I see for Alexandria have not changed much since the publishing of my first article on the name. Mega campuses continue to generate the bulk of rental revenue. That is a positive for the company as long as it maintains its very high occupancy rate. So far, this has not been an issue, and funding for the life science industry could be as much as $5 trillion.

Interest rates could remain elevated, making the cost of issuance more pronounced than in the prior decade. However, with Alexandria’s fixed rates and very long-dated repayment schedule in place, this is less of an issue for the company. If rates decline substantially, the company can refinance into a lower rate.

Finally, while the market has paid a very high multiple for the stock in past years, there is no guarantee that it will in the future. If the multiple settles around the current level, then total returns would be slightly less than I have forecasted here. Still, 6% FFO growth and the 4%+ dividend yield would still allow for a high rate of return even if the valuation were to be stagnant.

Final Thoughts

I have been very bullish on Alexandria since first looking deeply at the company in July. The most recent earnings report has worked to reinforce this rating, as Alexandria is a top name in the healthcare REIT space. It has several factors working in its favor, including a presence near important mega campuses, high occupancy and rental collection rates, a very reasonable valuation, and a higher-than-usual dividend yield.

If the market rerates the stock at a higher valuation to go along with 6% FFO growth and the 4%+ yield, Alexandria is likely to produce at least double-digit total returns. For these reasons, I continue to rate shares of Alexandria as a strong buy.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Boeing Loses $6 Billion
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Is all the bad news priced in now for Boeing? (0:15) Stocks slump on chip weakness. (2:35) Nvidia mea culpa. (3:58)

The following is an abridged transcript:

Our top story so far, is Boeing (NYSE:BA) at a bottom?

There’s a case to be made that all the bad news it priced into the plane-maker’s stock, with shares down slightly after Boeing reported a $6.7 billion quarterly loss.

In line with an earlier warning, Boeing reported a net loss of $9.97 a share on revenue of $17.8 billion. The company burned through $1.3 billion in cash, leaving it with about $10.5 billion at the end of September.

CEO Kelly Ortberg says the company has lots of work ahead before it can build a newly designed plane.

“We need to know what’s going on, not only with our products, but with our people,” he said in a memo to employees. “And most importantly, we need to prevent the festering of issues and work better together to identify, fix, and understand the root cause.”

He said the company must fix a broken culture, get smaller and improve execution on new plane models that have been delayed for years. Ortberg’s memo included remarks for his first earnings call.

Boeing in the past six years has worked to overcome two deadly crashes of its popular 737 Max jet, the pandemic’s effect on air travel and global supply chains, a criminal conviction, factory delays, a blowout of a door panel on an Alaska Airlines flight and a strike by 33,000 machinists.

SA Investing Group Leader Dhierin Bechai says: "A 1% decline in sales for the entire company is not extremely bad, but it should be noted that the biggest part of the impact on the work stoppage will likely be felt in Q4. The results were not pretty, but with the challenges Boeing faces, a $2 billion free cash flow burn, while a lot, is actually quite OK."

Also reporting this morning, AT&T (T) followed suit like its peer Verizon (VZ) and posted a small revenue miss and an adjusted profit beat for the third quarter while reaffirming its forecast for the full year.

But for the full year, it continues to expect adjusted EPS in the $2.15-$2.25 range (midpoint $2.20) vs $2.20 consensus, on wireless service revenue growth of 3%. Broadband revenue growth is seen up +7% and adjusted EBITDA growth is in the 3% range.

In today’s trading, Wall Street slumped on Wednesday, weighed down by a slide in technology and consumer discretionary stocks and a post-earnings plunge in some high-profile companies.

Meanwhile, a continued bond sell-off on bets that the Federal Reserve would not be as aggressive with rate cuts as previously expected also weighed on equities.

Technology was slammed as chip stocks fell, led by a big decrease in Arm (ARM). The British chip designer canceled a license deal that allows Qualcomm (QCOM) to use its intellectual property.

Among other active stocks, Texas Instruments (TXN) got some buying love after third-quarter beat as Wall Street analysts had largely positive views on the stock.

Benchmark reiterated its Buy rating and its $230 price target on the stock.

The analysts believe Texas Instruments elevated internal inventory should allow it to aggressively compete for sockets it was forced to pass on during the period of COVID-related supply constraints. That should continue the momentum it is currently enjoying in its non-auto and Industrial businesses. It also allows the company to flex its supply and capacity strengths as its broader core markets begin to see shipments return to consumption levels next year.

And Enphase Energy (ENPH) plunged after reporting a wide miss on Q3 adjusted earnings and a 31% Y/Y decline in revenues, while also guiding Q4 revenues well below consensus.

For Q4, Enphase issued downside guidance for revenues of $360 million to $400 million vs. $433.5 million analyst consensus, with non-GAAP gross margin seen in a range of 49%-52%.

In other news of note, Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said the design flaw that caused production of its Blackwell line of GPUs to be delayed is now fixed and that it was entirely the semiconductor giant's fault.

"We had a design flaw in Blackwell," Huang said in Denmark, according to Reuters. "It was functional, but the design flaw caused the yield to be low. It was 100% Nvidia's fault."

Huang added that Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), which manufactures processors for Nvidia, helped the company recover from the yield issues and "resume the manufacturing of Blackwell at an incredible pace."

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, billionaire hedge fund manager Paul Tudor Jones says he is long gold (XAUUSD:CUR), bitcoin (BTC-USD) and commodities, and also noted that the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite index (COMP:IND) was a "great" inflation hedge.

On CNBC he said the "playbook" for the way to reduce U.S. debt to GDP ratio is to "inflate your way out."

He said: "You have a small tax on the consumer, and you run interest rates below inflation and nominal growth above inflation."

"All roads lead to inflation. I am long gold. I am long bitcoin. I think commodities are so ridiculously underowned, so I'm long commodities."

"I think most young people find their inflation hedges via the Nasdaq, that's also been great. I probably have some basket of gold, bitcoin, commodities and Nasdaq, something like that. And I own zero fixed income.”
